notes: Continues the format from batches 1-3: 4 event-based clues per location, ordered hardest -> easiest, plus modernCountry, ancientRegion, and a didYouKnow fact.

clues: Paul had long wanted to visit this city and finally arrived there under armed guard.
He spent two years under house arrest here, still preaching and writing letters.
Paul wrote his letter to the believers already living in this city years before he ever arrived.
It was the capital of the empire that ruled over nearly the entire Biblical world in the New Testament.
locations.didYouKnow: Tradition holds that both Paul and Peter were eventually martyred in Rome under Emperor Nero.

clues: Paul's ship wrecked on this island's shore during a violent storm while he was being taken to Rome.
A viper bit Paul here while he was gathering firewood, but he suffered no harm.
The islanders first thought he was a murderer, then decided he must be a god.
He healed the sick father of the island's chief official during his stay here.
locations.didYouKnow: Malta still celebrates 'St. Paul's Shipwreck' as a public holiday every February.

clues: Paul had a night vision here of a man from Macedonia begging him to come help.
That vision redirected his entire missionary journey from Asia into Europe.
A young man named Eutychus fell asleep during one of Paul's long sermons here and fell from a window.
Paul raised Eutychus back to life after he fell three stories to his apparent death.
locations.didYouKnow: Troas sat near the ruins of ancient Troy, the setting of Homer's Iliad.

clues: Paul called the elders of a nearby city to meet him here rather than delay his journey by visiting in person.
He gave them an emotional farewell speech, telling them they would never see his face again.
The elders wept and embraced him before he sailed on toward Jerusalem.
It sat on the coast near Ephesus, in the Roman province of Asia.
locations.didYouKnow: Miletus was once a great center of Greek philosophy, home to some of the earliest known scientific thinkers.

clues: The Jewish community here was described as more noble than those in a nearby city, for testing Paul's teaching against Scripture.
They examined the Scriptures daily to see if what Paul said was true.
Paul had to be quickly sent away from here once opponents from Thessalonica arrived stirring up trouble.
It sat in the Roman province of Macedonia, in what is now northern Greece.
locations.didYouKnow: The phrase 'noble Bereans' is still used today to describe someone who carefully checks teaching against Scripture rather than accepting it blindly.

clues: Paul and Barnabas made this island their first stop on their first missionary journey.
A sorcerer here tried to turn a Roman official away from the faith and was struck blind.
It was the homeland of Barnabas, Paul's traveling companion.
It's a large island in the eastern Mediterranean Sea.
locations.didYouKnow: Cyprus is where Saul is first referred to by his Roman name, Paul, in the book of Acts.

clues: Paul and Barnabas arrived here after sailing from Cyprus on their first missionary journey.
John Mark left the group and returned to Jerusalem from this city, later causing a sharp disagreement between Paul and Barnabas.
Paul preached here again on his return journey before sailing home.
It sat inland from the southern coast of Asia Minor, in the region of Pamphylia.
locations.didYouKnow: John Mark's departure here later became such a point of contention that Paul and Barnabas split into separate missionary teams over it.

clues: Abraham made a covenant here with a local king over a dispute about a well.
He named the site after an oath, its name meaning 'well of the oath' or 'well of seven.'
Isaac later dug wells and had his own covenant meeting here as well.
It marked the southern boundary of Israel in the phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba.'
locations.didYouKnow: The phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba' was the ancient equivalent of saying 'from one end of the country to the other.'

clues: This city marked the northernmost point of Israelite territory.
The tribe of Dan conquered and resettled this city, renaming it after their tribal ancestor.
King Jeroboam set up one of two golden calves here to keep people from worshiping in Jerusalem.
It sat at the base of Mount Hermon, near one of the main sources of the Jordan River.
locations.didYouKnow: One of the main headwater springs of the Jordan River still flows from this site today.

clues: Jacob and his father-in-law Laban made a covenant here and set up a stone heap as a witness.
The Israelites gathered here to fast and repent under Samuel's leadership.
Samuel judged Israel from this location for many years.
Saul was chosen and publicly presented here as Israel's first king.
locations.didYouKnow: The name Mizpah is often invoked today in the phrase 'the Lord watch between me and thee,' drawn from Jacob and Laban's covenant here.

clues: Saul disguised himself and traveled here in desperation the night before his final battle.
He sought out a medium here, despite having banned such practices himself.
The medium summoned the spirit of Samuel, who predicted Saul's defeat and death.
It sat near Mount Gilboa, where Saul would die the very next day.
locations.didYouKnow: This event is one of the most debated passages in the Old Testament regarding what actually appeared to Saul.

clues: King Saul and his sons died in battle against the Philistines on this mountain.
David composed a mournful lament after hearing the news of their deaths here.
That lament famously repeats the line, 'How the mighty have fallen.'
It sits in northern Israel, overlooking the Jezreel Valley.
locations.didYouKnow: David's lament asked that dew and rain never again fall on these mountains, as a mark of mourning.

clues: King Ahab coveted a vineyard here that belonged to a man named Naboth.
Queen Jezebel arranged to have Naboth falsely accused and killed to seize his land.
Elijah confronted Ahab here, prophesying doom for both him and Jezebel.
It sat in a fertile valley overlooked by Mount Gilboa.
locations.didYouKnow: Jezebel's own name later became a byword for wickedness, largely because of what happened in this valley.

clues: This heavily fortified city guarded one of the most important trade and military routes in the ancient world.
King Josiah of Judah was killed in battle here against an Egyptian pharaoh.
Its name, combined with a Hebrew word for 'mountain,' gives us a famous word from Revelation.
It's the traditional site referenced as 'Armageddon,' the location of a final great battle.
locations.didYouKnow: Armageddon literally means 'Mountain of Megiddo,' referencing this real archaeological site in northern Israel.

clues: This city's inhabitants tricked Joshua into a peace treaty by disguising themselves as weary travelers from far away.
Five kings attacked this city for making peace with Israel, prompting Joshua to come to its defense.
During that battle, Joshua asked God to make the sun stand still in the sky.
It sat in the hill country near Jerusalem, within the territory of Benjamin.
locations.didYouKnow: The book of Joshua explicitly notes that there was no day like it before or since, when the Lord listened to a human voice.

clues: Israel suffered a surprising defeat here shortly after their victory at Jericho.
The defeat was traced back to a man named Achan, who had secretly kept forbidden plunder.
Once the sin was dealt with, Joshua used an ambush strategy to finally conquer this city.
It was the second city Israel took after entering the Promised Land.
locations.didYouKnow: The city's name, Ai, simply means 'the ruin' in Hebrew, likely a description rather than an original proper name.

clues: Abraham pitched his tents among the great trees here for much of his life.
Three visitors, understood to include the Lord himself, appeared to him here and promised Sarah a son.
Sarah laughed to herself upon overhearing the promise, since she was long past childbearing age.
It sat near Hebron, in the hill country of Canaan.
locations.didYouKnow: The 'great trees of Mamre' are mentioned as a landmark significant enough that Abraham built an altar there.

clues: David was given this town as a refuge while fleeing King Saul.
It was raided and burned by Amalekites while David and his men were away.
David pursued the raiders and recovered everything and everyone that had been taken.
It sat on the border between Israelite and Philistine territory in the south.
locations.didYouKnow: David was living in Ziklag under Philistine protection at the very time King Saul died at Mount Gilboa.

clues: David hid in caves in this rugged wilderness area while being pursued by King Saul.
In one of these caves, David secretly cut off a piece of Saul's robe rather than kill him.
It's known for its natural springs in the middle of an otherwise harsh desert landscape.
It sits along the western shore of the Dead Sea.
locations.didYouKnow: En Gedi remains a lush desert oasis today, with waterfalls and wildlife rare anywhere else nearby.

clues: A Jewish woman was brought here to become queen after winning a kingdom-wide beauty search.
Her cousin Mordecai discovered a plot to assassinate the king while sitting at this city's gate.
A royal official named Haman plotted here to destroy the entire Jewish people.
The queen, Esther, risked her life by approaching the king here uninvited to save her people.
locations.didYouKnow: Susa served as one of the primary capitals of the vast Persian Empire, the same empire that had earlier allowed Jewish exiles to return home.
